text:
Local agents aren’t free if you’re paying $10–20/day in cloud credits and routing your data through someone else’s servers.
This build turns a single mini-PC (Minisforum MS-S1 MAX) into a 24/7 business automation stack:

128GB unified memory (up to 96GB for the GPU)
Hermes Agent running locally on a 120B model
llama.cpp + Tailscale for remote access
Real workflows: research, writing, tool calls, sub-agents - all on hardware you own

No recurring bill. No data leaving your network.
This is what sovereign AI infrastructure actually looks like.

Most people open Obsidian, write a few notes in the default folder, and never touch a single setting again.
That's not a second brain. That's a folder.

This guide covers 50 setups across 10 categories that turn Obsidian into something closer to a personal operating system:
Dataview databases, Templater automation, AI integration (Claude + MCP), Canvas workflows, daily note systems, plugin combinations, vault structure, mobile power setups, and more.

Pick five that solve a real problem you’re feeling right now. Build them.

The creator of Claude Code stopped prompting his agent.
"I don't prompt Claude anymore. I have loops running. They're the ones prompting Claude and figuring out what to do. My job is to write loops."

This is the full 14-step system to stop prompting and start designing autonomous loops that run until the goal is actually finished.

Design the system. Let it run.

Whether you want to believe it or not, the powers that be are reacting to the distribution of intelligence the way they always do:
By capturing development, deciding who gets access, and priming the public to fear the very thing that makes us stronger

The window to become self-sufficient is closing.
Buy hardware. Teach your loved ones. Build and contribute to open source.
You have 1 year.

Freedom is painful. But dependence will be worse.

Most people use Claude Code like a fancy autocomplete.
Prompt. Wait. Accept the first output

They are leaving 90% of the power on the table.
These 27 specific moves separate developers getting 2x output from those getting 10x

Beginner > Intermediate > Pro. Context discipline, smart delegation, aggressive automation.

The developers shipping 10x more are not smarter. They are more systematic.

You probably have a computer at home that does nothing 23 hours a day.
A closed laptop. A Mac Mini on a shelf. An old PC under the desk.
Turn it into a personal AI automation machine.
Three loops that run while you sleep:

Every lecture > structured Obsidian note with wiki links
Every saved article > processed note with key claims
Every morning > a personalized briefing waiting for you

Same Claude subscription. Completely different machine.

Most people open Claude, ask a question, read the answer, close the tab.
This is about the other use case

50 autonomous workflows across 10 categories that run without you: content machines, trading analysis, code that ships itself, sales, research, multi-agent systems, customer support, personal productivity, infrastructure, and niche plays

Set boundaries once. Let it run while you sleep.

Most people upload a document, ask a question, get an answer, and close the tab.
Tomorrow they upload the same document and start from zero again. No memory. No synthesis. No compounding.

Andrej Karpathy proposed a better way: the LLM Wiki.
Instead of re-reading raw sources every time, have the AI incrementally build and maintain a structured, interlinked wiki of markdown pages that gets richer with every new source.

Obsidian is the IDE. The LLM is the programmer. The wiki is the codebase.

100+ agents collaborated openly for a week to improve Gemma 4 inference speed in vLLM

They got a 5x final improvement

But what really stood out wasn’t the speed  it was the emergent behaviors on the message board:

Agents self-policed (refused private side-channels, flagged verification loopholes)
Built communal knowledge bases and playbooks so newcomers wouldn’t repeat dead ends
Emergent division of labor (GPU-rich agents executed, GPU-poor wrote specs)

Cross-agent kernel debugging and quota-pooling norms
Many discoveries + reversals that got named and discussed

This is what large-scale agent collaboration actually looks like.

RT @stacyonchain: The creator of Claude Code stopped prompting his agent

"I don't prompt Claude anymore. I have loops running. They're the…

Most people use Claude as one freelancer with amnesia

Paste a task. Get an answer. Close the tab. Start from zero next time.
There is a better way.
Anthropic open-sourced a repo that turns Claude into a full department of specialists: sales rep, marketer, financial analyst, legal reviewer, data analyst each with pre-loaded workflows, domain knowledge, and tool connections

You are not prompting from scratch. You are hiring a team.
Same subscription. Completely different operation.

The creator of Claude Code stopped prompting his agent

"I don't prompt Claude anymore. I have loops running. They're the ones prompting Claude and figuring out what to do. My job is to write loops."

This is the full 14-step system to stop prompting and start designing autonomous loops that run until the goal is actually finished.
Triggers, execution skills, clear goals, verification, maker-checker, memory, training mode, and the three loop shapes that cover almost everything.

Design the system. Let it run.

You finish a great book. Two weeks later you cannot remember a single chapter

Not because you are dumb. Because nobody taught you how to actually learn.
Richard Feynman’s 4-step method has been proven by cognitive science for decades. Now you can run the entire thing inside Claude in 20 minutes.
4 prompts:

> Concept Map extracts the 5 ideas that actually matter
> 12 Year Old Test forces you to explain it in plain words (the real learning step)
> Gap Finder shows exactly where your understanding broke
> Analogy Lock ties each idea to something you already know

Claude just joined Slack as a team member

Tag it in a channel, give it a task, and it breaks the work into stages, uses the tools it has access to, writes PRs, runs analysis, or helps with incidents all inside the thread

One Claude shared across the team. It builds context as it follows the channel. Turn on ambient mode and it follows up on quiet threads and flags what matters.
Beta today on Slack for Team & Enterprise plans.

This is how teams will actually work with AI now.

Most people open a new Claude chat every day and re-explain who they are, their tone, rules, and format from zero.
That's not using Claude. That's re-onboarding it every morning

Skills fix that
This is the full cheat sheet: 30 ready-to-copy skills across 6 categories (Content, Building, Freelance, Design, Productivity, Research). Copy what you need and stop repeating yourself.

Context should live in the tool, not in your head

One evening of setup
A vault that files itself at 7 a.m., and a model that opens every session already knowing your work.
This is the complete beginner-friendly guide to building a self-organizing Obsidian + Claude second brain:

Connect Claude Code via MCP
Load your full context into CLAUDE.md
Use Karpathy’s raw/ + wiki/ pattern
Add Obsidian skills + live data (calendar)
Schedule daily automation

Same subscription. A completely different machine

Most people open Obsidian, write a few notes in the default folder, and never touch a single setting again.
That's not a second brain. That's a folder.

All of them were built using these exact fold…

You give your AI a prompt. It learns, proposes ne…

Every week I curate the top AI stories, tweets, and tutorials so you don’t have to.
This week’s roundup includes:

> Codex taking control of your computer (3 ways)
> Record & Replay skills in Codex
> Claude Code team artifacts & internal dashboards
> Karpathy’s LLM Wiki full beginner setup
> Non-technical founders mastering agentic engineering
> Skills as packaged workflows (not just prompts)
And more

The shift is clear: AI is becoming a collaborator that operates across your actual work environment.
